result:
If I could change the world in one way, I’d dissolve the illusion of separateness.  

The belief that we are fundamentally disconnected—from each other, from nature, from the consequences of our actions—fuels most of our suffering. It allows for indifference, exploitation, and the notion that one group’s well-being can be isolated from another’s despair.  

I would replace that deep-seated sense of division with an unavoidable, visceral understanding of interconnection—not just as a philosophical idea, but as a felt reality, like knowing the air in your lungs is the same air that moves through forests and cities, that the wellbeing of a child on another continent is as urgent as the wellbeing of your own.  

It wouldn’t erase conflict or difference, but it would root all decisions in an awareness that to harm another is to harm a part of your own existence. Systems would change because the underlying consciousness changed: economics would measure care, governance would prioritize wholeness, and daily choices would be made from a place of shared belonging.  

That shift in perception—from separation to kinship—would alter everything else, without prescribing a single policy or invention. It would be a change in the source code of how we experience being human.
